items.title: What do people in middle-income countries think about cuts to U.S. humanitarian aid?
items.content_text: In 16 middle-income countries surveyed, many people are unsure how U.S. humanitarian aid cuts will affect them, though those with opinions lean negative.

items.title: U.S. foreign aid is plunging in second Trump administration
items.content_text: U.S. foreign aid fell to its lowest inflation-adjusted level in over two decades in fiscal 2025, dropping to $47.3 billion — more than $24 billion less than the prior year.

items.title: How global energy production is changing, in 6 charts
items.content_text: Fossil fuels still dominate global electricity, but wind and solar are surging. In 2025, renewables outpaced coal worldwide for the first time, supplying 34% of electricity.

items.title: We’re Squandering LEDs’ Potential to Save Our Night Skies
items.content_text: Bad decisions have turned efficient technology into a driver of light pollution. There is a better way

items.title: Majorities of Americans say key financial milestones are harder for today’s young adults to reach
items.content_text: Most Americans say it’s harder for young adults to find a job, buy a house, pay for college and save for their future than it was for their parents' generation.

items.title: Mexicans view President Claudia Sheinbaum favorably, but less so than in 2025
items.content_text: Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum remains popular, but her favorability has dropped to 64%. Views of the economy and democracy are also declining.

items.title: How we measured Americans’ engagement in public life
items.content_text: This piece explains how researchers measured Americans' engagement in public life using multimode surveys, address-based sampling and cluster analysis.

items.title: How Americans Are Engaged With News, Politics, Religion and Civic Life
items.content_text: Americans’ engagement with others is not one-dimensional. Instead, they sort into four distinct groups that reflect different patterns of participation in society.

items.title: Younger and older Americans engage in public life in different ways
items.content_text: Older adults are more likely to participate in civic activities like voting, donating or attending religious services, while younger adults engage more in online spaces.

items.title: Democrats and Republicans engage in public life in similar ways
items.content_text: Each of the two major parties includes people who are deeply involved in public life – across politics, community, religion and news – and those who are less so.

items.title: In conversation with Innovate Australia: Peter Kasprzak on supporting the AI for Good Innovation Factory Australia Local Chapter
items.content_text: The AI for Good Innovation Factory reaches its Grand Finale in Geneva each summer, following a year of activity across a large-scale international network. Regional chapters across multiple continents are hosted by organisations with deep knowledge of their local innovation ecosystems, helping to identify promising founders and select the strongest solutions to present before expert juries. With chapters already active in places including India, Catalonia and Malta, the network continues to expand, with Innovate Australia leading the initiative’s local chapter in Australia.

items.title: Day 4 at the AI for Good Global Summit: Women's leadership, education and human progress close the 2026 edition
items.content_text: GENEVA, July 10, 2026 - The AI for Good Global Summit concluded following four days of sessions that demonstrated the widespread application of artificial intelligence in a variety of sectors, including health and agriculture, mobility, education, creative industries, and public policy. The summit also raised awareness of quantum technologies, robotics, and the standards and infrastructure required to facilitate their widespread deployment.

items.title: AI for Good's Youth Zone Wraps Up With a Focus on Responsible Design
items.content_text: GENEVA, 10 July 2026 — Today marked the end of the programme at the AI for Good Global Summit 2026's Youth Zone, which revisited a number of sessions from earlier in the week, and introduced two new sessions on safety and creative problem-solving.

items.title: The Robotics for Good Youth Challenge 2026 comes to an end as winners are awarded
items.content_text: Connect with 55K+ AI enthusiasts

items.title: Top 3 things happening on Day 4 of the AI for Good Global Summit 2026
items.content_text: The Robotics for Good Youth Challenge Grand Finale will reach its conclusion on the Youth Stage from 14:10 to 14:45. After four days of competition, the challenge will come to an end, closing a season that opened the Youth Zone's programme on 7 July.

items.title: Fifteen minutes to convince the committee: meet the ITU Kaleidoscope 2026 winners
items.content_text: The 16th edition of the ITU Kaleidoscope academic conference wrapped up today in Geneva, after three days of peer-reviewed research presentations on AI and frontier technologies. The title of this conference, held under the auspices of the AI for Good Global Summit 2026, aptly reflects the nature of the entire programme: a weave of diverse perspectives emerging from the exchange among researchers from universities, industry and research institutions around the world to discuss how AI, quantum, space and strategic foresight can contribute to advancing sustainable and inclusive development.

items.title: How Americans are engaged with news, politics, religion and civic life
items.content_text: Numbers, Facts and Trends Shaping Your World

items.title: OII | The New Era of Lawyerless Contracting
items.content_text: Contract generators, platforms, and AI are reshaping consumer contracts.  Luka Nenadic explains how and highlights the potential pitfalls and policy implications.

items.title: OII | How social media interoperability could restore user choice
items.content_text: Social networking interoperability is a leading policy proposal that gives users a real choice of platform providers. New evidence shows it only works if regulators get the specifics right, argues Philipp Riederle, doctoral researcher, OII.

items.title: OII | UK adults increasingly seek emotional support and companionship from AI, new report finds
items.content_text: New analysis from researchers at the Oxford Internet Institute reveals that users of artificial intelligence tools are increasingly turning to them for personal support, emotional advice, and companionship.

items.title: OII | AI-powered social media can subtly manipulate opinion at scale, new study finds
items.content_text: AI tools used to generate, edit or contextualise social media posts can introduce hidden biases that spread through online networks and shape public opinion, according to new research from the OII and the Hasso Plattner Institute.

items.title: OII | Keeping Europe’s Technological Choices Open
items.content_text: In an age of AI chokepoints, Europe’s choices will depend on the capacities it builds now. The OII's Johanna Ballesteros and TUM Think Tank's DR Nicklas Berild Lundblad explain why.

items.title: OII | Rethinking EU AI policy: why public subsidies for AI should deliver real wellbeing
items.content_text: A new working paper by OII researchers argues that EU technology policy should hold AI companies to account for actual public wellbeing, not just reduced risk.

items.title: OII | Expert Comment: Is banning under-16s from social media protection or exclusion?
items.content_text: The OII's Dr Vicki Nash warns that a social media ban risks leaving teenagers 'protected' but disempowered, cut off from news, learning and support at the very moment the UK is preparing to give 16-year-olds the vote.

items.title: OII | OII researchers head to FAccT 2026
items.content_text: Researchers and DPhil students from the Oxford Internet Institute are set to attend the Association of Computing Machinery (ACM) Conference on Fairness, Accountability and Transparency (FAccT) in Montréal, from 25-28 June 2026.

items.title: OII | The efficiency paradox in EU data centre policy
items.content_text: New EU reporting rules for data centre energy and water use may look like progress, but loopholes risk undermining genuine environmental accountability.

items.title: OII | Friendly AI chatbots make more mistakes and tell people what they want to hear, study finds
items.content_text: New Oxford research shows that training chatbots to sound warmer makes them up to 30% less accurate, and 40% more likely to validate users' false beliefs.
